claims.claim_text: The opening retail-GPU autoscaling outage is presented as a real operational incident.
claims.span_hint: lines 29-31 | section: Why This Module Matters
claims.anchor_text: A retail company launched a recommendation feature that worked perfectly during a small pilot, then failed during the holiday traffic surge. The application Pods scaled, the frontend remained healthy, and the database had spare capacity, yet the recommendation endpoint became slow and then unavailable. The platform team eventually found the real problem: the model server needed GPUs, the cluster autoscaler was only adding CPU nodes, and half the inference replicas were stuck Pending while users waited for product suggestions that never arrived.
claims.suggested_rewrite: A recommendation service can pass an early pilot yet fail under heavier traffic when inference replicas need GPUs that the cluster cannot supply quickly enough.
claims.rationale: This is an unattributed outage-style anecdote, so the specific scenario should be collapsed to the general operational lesson.

claims.claim_text: The sentence about what KCNA does and does not expect from candidates makes a curriculum-specific claim.
claims.span_hint: line 33 | section: Why This Module Matters
claims.anchor_text: KCNA does not expect you to become a machine learning engineer or install every tool in the ecosystem.
claims.suggested_rewrite: You do not need to become a machine learning engineer or install every tool in the ecosystem to benefit from this module.
claims.rationale: The KCNA page supports foundational Kubernetes scope, but not this precise negative expectation claim about AI/ML tooling.

claims.claim_text: After the vendor device plugin is installed, Kubernetes exposes schedulable GPU resources such as `nvidia.com/gpu`, and Pods consume them through `resources.limits`.
claims.span_hint: lines 87-89, 107-117, 682, 721-730 | sections: 2. How GPUs Become Schedulable Kubernetes Resources; Hands-On Exercise solutions
claims.rationale: The Kubernetes GPU scheduling documentation directly covers plugin installation, `nvidia.com/gpu`, and the `limits`-based request semantics.

claims.claim_text: Extended device resources are integer, non-overcommittable resources, and a Pod requesting them can only schedule onto a node that has enough advertised devices available.
claims.span_hint: lines 79-81, 89, 451-463, 706-718 | sections: 1. Kubernetes Is Useful for AI/ML; 2. How GPUs Become Schedulable Kubernetes Resources; Worked Example; Hands-On Exercise solutions
claims.rationale: The device plugin documentation explicitly states that extended resources are integer-only, cannot be overcommitted, and can only schedule where the request can be satisfied.

claims.claim_text: Node labels or selectors steer workloads to special-hardware nodes, while taints keep other Pods off those nodes unless they tolerate the taint.
claims.span_hint: lines 117, 155, 467-479, 592-594, 645-648, 755 | sections: 2. How GPUs Become Schedulable Kubernetes Resources; Worked Example; Common Mistakes; Quiz 8 answer; Hands-On Exercise extra practice
claims.rationale: The taints and tolerations documentation explicitly describes repelling Pods from dedicated or special-hardware nodes and pairing taints with labels or affinity when needed.

claims.claim_text: NVIDIA time-slicing allows multiple workloads to share a GPU with weaker isolation, while MIG-capable configurations expose hardware-partitioned GPU slices on supported hardware.
claims.span_hint: lines 113-115, 157, 597 | sections: 2. How GPUs Become Schedulable Kubernetes Resources; Common Mistakes
claims.rationale: The NVIDIA device plugin README documents time-slicing behavior, its weaker isolation model, and MIG-related resource strategies.

claims.claim_text: The chart-refactor image-classifier incident is presented as a real war story.
claims.span_hint: line 159 | section: 2. How GPUs Become Schedulable Kubernetes Resources
claims.anchor_text: War story: a team once moved a prototype image classifier from a single development node to a shared cluster and kept the same `nodeSelector` but accidentally dropped the GPU limit during a chart refactor. The Pod still landed on the GPU node because the selector matched, so the deployment looked correct during a quick review. Under load, the container failed when the model runtime could not find an assigned device, and ordinary CPU workloads also started landing on the expensive node because the taint policy was inconsistent. The fix was not one magic label; the fix was to make selectors, taints, tolerations, and extended resource requests tell the same story.
claims.suggested_rewrite: A team can misconfigure GPU placement by keeping node-selection rules while dropping the actual GPU resource request, which leads to confusing runtime failures and wasted accelerator capacity.
claims.rationale: This is an unattributed operational anecdote, so the fabricated deployment story should be reduced to the underlying lesson.

claims.claim_text: Kubernetes Jobs represent one-off tasks that run to completion and are a natural fit for finite training or batch-inference workloads.
claims.span_hint: lines 202, 206, 218, 563, 593, 612, 630 | sections: 3. Training, Fine-Tuning, Batch Inference, and Real-Time Inference; Decision Framework; Common Mistakes; Quiz 2 and 5 answers
claims.rationale: The Jobs documentation explicitly defines Jobs as one-off tasks that run to completion and track successful completions.

claims.claim_text: Volcano adds batch-scheduling features such as gang scheduling and fair-share scheduling for AI/ML workloads.
claims.span_hint: line 228, line 354, lines 517, 568, 582, 618 | sections: 4. Distributed Training Needs Scheduling Guarantees; 6. Ecosystem Tools; Patterns & Anti-Patterns; Decision Framework; Did You Know; Quiz 3 answer
claims.rationale: Volcano's own documentation is the right primary source for its gang and fair-share scheduling features, but `volcano.sh` is off the current allowlist.

claims.claim_text: Volcano queues are a core resource-management concept for multi-tenant batch scheduling.
claims.span_hint: line 228, line 354, line 517, line 568 | sections: 4. Distributed Training Needs Scheduling Guarantees; 6. Ecosystem Tools; Patterns & Anti-Patterns; Decision Framework
claims.rationale: The Volcano queue documentation is the primary source for queue semantics, but that host is off allowlist.

claims.claim_text: Startup probes delay readiness and liveness checks until initialization succeeds, and failed readiness removes a Pod from regular traffic while the container keeps running.
claims.span_hint: lines 288, 331, 333, 337, 584, 596, 636 | sections: 5. Model Serving Adds Latency, Rollout, and Privacy Trade-Offs; Did You Know; Common Mistakes; Quiz 6 answer
claims.rationale: The probes documentation directly explains startup-probe gating and failed-readiness behavior.

claims.claim_text: Kubeflow is a modular foundation of tools for AI platforms on Kubernetes that can be used project-by-project or as a fuller reference platform.
claims.span_hint: lines 343, 349, 374, 598, 630 | sections: 6. Ecosystem Tools; Tool table; Common Mistakes; Quiz 5 answer
claims.rationale: The Kubeflow repository overview explicitly describes Kubeflow as a foundation of tools and an AI reference platform on Kubernetes.

claims.claim_text: KServe provides a Kubernetes inference platform with request-based autoscaling, advanced deployment patterns such as canaries, and multi-framework model serving.
claims.span_hint: lines 345, 350, 372-376, 565, 630 | sections: 6. Ecosystem Tools; Tool table; Architecture check answer; Decision Framework; Quiz 5 answer
claims.rationale: The KServe repository README explicitly describes the platform, its autoscaling, advanced deployment, and multi-framework inference features.

claims.claim_text: Ray provides a distributed runtime and libraries for training, data processing, and serving, including Kubernetes deployment support.
claims.span_hint: lines 345, 351, 567, 574 | sections: 6. Ecosystem Tools; Tool table; Decision Framework; final workflow paragraph
claims.rationale: The Ray repository overview directly describes Ray's distributed runtime and its Train, Data, and Serve libraries, and notes Kubernetes support.

claims.claim_text: vLLM is a high-throughput, memory-efficient inference and serving engine for large language models.
claims.span_hint: lines 345, 352, 378, 574 | sections: 6. Ecosystem Tools; Tool table; runtime-choice paragraph; final workflow paragraph
claims.rationale: The vLLM repository directly describes the project as a high-throughput, memory-efficient LLM serving engine.

claims.claim_text: The NVIDIA GPU Operator automates GPU-node software components including drivers, the device plugin, container runtime, node labeling, and monitoring.
claims.span_hint: lines 353, 376, 592, 730, 752 | sections: 6. Ecosystem Tools; GPU operations paragraph; Common Mistakes; Hands-On Exercise solution and checklist
claims.rationale: The GPU Operator README explicitly lists the automated GPU software components it manages on Kubernetes.

further_reading.title: Kubernetes: Schedule GPUs
further_reading.why_relevant: This is the primary upstream reference for how Kubernetes exposes and schedules GPU resources.

further_reading.title: Kubernetes: Device Plugins
further_reading.why_relevant: It explains how specialized hardware becomes visible to the kubelet and the scheduler.

further_reading.title: Kubernetes: Liveness, Readiness, and Startup Probes
further_reading.why_relevant: It directly supports the model-serving readiness and startup behavior discussed in the module.

further_reading.title: KServe Project Overview
further_reading.why_relevant: It is the clearest allowlisted upstream summary of production inference features on Kubernetes.

notes: Skipped quiz prompts, hands-on steps, checklists, code blocks, and most pedagogical analogies unless they contained concrete factual claims. Repeated claims were consolidated into single entries with multi-span hints. The two anecdotal 'war story' openings and the KCNA-specific expectation sentence should be rewritten rather than cited as-is.
